titleStmt.title: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark) to the Joint Chiefs of Staff

head.hi: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark) to the Joint Chiefs of Staff
head.note: Marginal notation on the source text indicated that the Secretary of State saw this telegram.

div.p: CX 50050. References: A. JCS 910484.Dated June 5, p. 310. B. JCS 910520.In this telegram, dated June 5, the JCS informed Clark of the recent Indian-Chinese discussions on the POW question in Peking and sent him the text of the Eden draft formula for a settlement of the issue with the U.S. Government’s suggested revisions (795.00/6–2852). C. CX 68858.Dated May 22, p. 219. D. C 69351.Dated May 31, p. 270. This msg in 2 parts.

list.item: Although we have carefully analyzed reference A, and although we recognize that the concept of rescreening by neutral nations may offer a possible chance to obtain a successful armistice, I consider it imperative that such action does not jeopardize the early completion of the initial screening program which was interrupted by the violent action of certain compound leaders on Koje-Do.

I am proceeding as rapidly as possible to achieve uncontested control of Koje-Do in accordance with the plan as set forth in reference C
and initiated on 10 June. However, I consider the maintenance of law and order on Koje-Do after the completion of this operation, using the minimum of guard personnel, to be dependent upon the removal of the anti-Communists from the Communist compounds. Experience has shown that the number of incidents in compounds containing POWs with like political beliefs is much lower than in compounds with inmates of mixed political beliefs. Our completion of the initial screening of all POWs and CIs as soon as practicable has the following additional advantages:
a.
In case the Communists agree to an armistice on the basis of the 28 April proposal, we will be prepared to execute immediate exchange with final rosters prepared on the basis of the completed screening. We cannot do this now.
b.
We will have a final accurate figure of the number to be repatriated which we can justify, whereas we are now vulnerable to an attack on our 70,000 figure (even as an estimate) since only about 39,000 of the 70,000 have been selected for repatriation through a proper individual screening.
c.
After the completion of initial screening the conduct of any rescreening could be executed with less chance of violence because the hardcore Communist POWs and CIs would not be involved.
d.
Does not appear to jeopardize the possibility of obtaining agreement on a bilateral rescreening by neutrals nor of completing a unilateral rescreening program.
e.
If the completion of screening results in a figure appreciably higher than 70,000, there is a slight possibility that the Communists may agree to the revised figure.

I consider the foregoing advantages to completing the initial screening are over-riding and require that this operation be completed without delay before the question of rescreening can prevent such action being taken. Accordingly, I intend to accomplish screening of the individuals in the unscreened compounds at the earliest possible date after uncontested control is gained. I have directed the immediate preparation of careful plans for the completion of the initial screening with particular reference to the essential precautions which must be taken to avoid the charge of forced screening.

In view of the above, I strongly recommend that I be given authority at a time of my choosing to complete the screening program for POWs and CIs who have not been screened to date. Considering our previous exchange of messages resulting in agreement to discontinue screening, I feel it necessary to secure your approval of this program. I further recommend that the action on the neutral rescreening concept not be allowed to interfere with the completion of screening.

We can see no cogent objection to a rescreening by or under the observation of a group of neutral nations of those POWs who have stated that they would violently resist repatriation, provided we are
able and permitted to first accomplish an initial screening of all POWs for reasons as stated in part 1. The proposed rescreening might assist in obtaining an armistice. Once initial screening is completed and we have a firm, final figure of those to be repatriated, any rescreening of non-repatriates might increase our final figure and hence the concept might be acceptable to the Communists because all advantage of rescreening accrues to them. If a rescreening of non-repatriates produces a material increase in the number of pro-Communist POWs, we might expect some criticism of our initial screening from Allies and the US public. However, I do not consider that this can offset the advantage of securing an armistice.

The likelihood of Communist acceptance of any rescreening by a group of neutral nations as a basis for an armistice is doubtful. I believe that in reality the Communists are fully aware that we were impartial in our screening and that the current figure of approximately 70,000 is generally valid. They must also realize that a rescreening, even conducted by or under a group of neutral nations, will still leave an impressive number of POWs who will violently resist repatriation. Their propaganda and moral defeat would still remain an ugly fact in their eyes. Further, they have repeatedly declared the very act of screening to be illegal and unacceptable. While I believe they are more influenced by figures than principles, their constant denunciations of the screening concept has placed them in a more and more uncompromising position from which they will find it progressively more difficult to extricate themselves. If the Communists agree to participate in any manner in any rescreening program, I would consider that an armistice was imminent.

Part I H of reference A, further amplified in reference B, sets forth a possible means of achieving an armistice via diplomatic and circuitous channels. Since these means are outside the scope of my command and progress may be long delayed, I have not considered them in developing my proposed plan. I believe it better to develop as sound a procedure as possible within the means and facilities available to us, while at the same time keeping such plans flexible enough so that any adjustment can be made to fit into developments which might arise from unofficial diplomatic representation to the Chinese Government.

Part II of reference A leaves some question in my mind as to whether the composition of the group of neutral nations to accomplish the rescreening would be determined bilaterally or unilaterally. Reference B indicates that such a group would be determined bilaterally. I feel that this is dependent upon whether the rescreening is to be accomplished under the observation of the group of neutral nations or by such a group. A bilateral agreement as to the neutral nations to conduct or observe the rescreening will be more palatable to the Communists. If a bilateral agreement on neutral nations to participate in the rescreening
is to be sought, I consider that we must be prepared to accept the enemy’s nomination of Communist nations. However, I would strongly oppose the acceptance of Russia. In this connection it might be better to refer to the nations concerned as non-participating rather than neutral. I believe, rather than have the armistice delegation introduce the subject of specific neutral nations across the conference table and then attempt to secure bilateral agreement, that prior diplomatic representation would probably avoid long discussion and delay and allow us to better maintain our firm position in plenary session.

The participation of Communist-dominated countries as members of the group of neutral nations to accomplish the rescreening directly affects my opinion as to whether such rescreening should be accomplished by them or only under their observation. If accomplished by them with Communist-dominated countries as members, it would afford them many opportunities to delay, obstruct, and influence the rescreening process. If accomplished under the observation of a neutral group such opportunities would not exist. I believe that we should attempt to secure Communist agreement for the rescreening to be only observed by the group of neutral nations. Further, I believe that we should resist efforts by our Allies which would permit any Communist-dominated country to participate in the rescreening other than as an observer. If the Communists fail to agree to participate in any rescreening, and if participating nations are non-Communist-dominated, it would then be preferable for the rescreening to actually be conducted by the group of neutral nations and observed by members of the United Nations Command.

In view of the foregoing, I propose that our plans to accomplish rescreening by a group of neutral nations proceed along the lines set forth below. Each successive proposal contributes less to the achievement of an armistice but tends to increase the support of our Allies for our position. The Communists may construe these steps to be a recession from our present position. Rescreening, however conducted, should provide for observation throughout by ICRC.
a.
After prior diplomatic agreement, if all possible has been obtained on specific neutral nations to accomplish the rescreening, propose in plenary session that a group of neutral nations to observe the rescreening be determined on a bilateral basis; that the rescreening to establish the validity of resultant number of persons to be repatriated be accomplished under the observation of the group of neutral nations; that prior to initiating the rescreening both sides agree in advance to abide by results, and that upon the conclusion of the rescreening both sides conclude the armistice on basis of results and on agreements thus far achieved on other issues; that with the foregoing understanding, a cease fire be executed prior to initiating of rescreening; that Military Armistice Commission and neutral nations supervisory personnel be assembled, organized and prepared to function upon signing of the armistice;
and, that representatives of the Chinese Communist and North Korean Army Forces and members of the UNC observe the rescreening. There would have to be a definite understanding as to the terms of the “cease fire.” The agreement might well include provision for the repatriation, prior to determination of results of rescreening, of those POWs of both sides who have indicated that they desire to be repatriated, such repatriation to be based on rosters to be exchanged prior to concluding the agreement to abide by results. Although the term rescreening has been employed above, the agreement would be couched in such terms as to insure that the group of neutral nations would screen the POWs of both sides who have stated they would resist repatriation. Such is deemed necessary to insure the return of the maximum number of United Nations POWs being held by the Communists. The same provisions to insure the safe return of United Nations POWs might be desirable in the proposal as set forth in the following subparagraph.
b.
If the Communists fail to agree to the foregoing, we would then make the proposal set forth in 6a above, but excluding that portion pertaining to any agreements to abide by the results of the rescreening, to the cease fire, and to the organization and establishment of the Military Armistice Commission and the Neutral Nations Supervisory Commission.
c.
If the Communists fail to agree to either of the foregoing, we would inform them that we are proceeding with the rescreening of those individuals who have stated they would forcibly resist repatriation, that this rescreening is to be conducted by a group of neutral nations (such as Switzerland, Sweden, India), and that representatives of the Chinese Communists and North Korean Army Forces would be welcome to observe the rescreening.
d.
We would then proceed on a specified date to initiate the rescreening with or without representatives of the Chinese Communist and North Korean Army Forces present. This would be conducted by the group of neutral nations and not just under their observation.

If the rescreening were conducted in accordance with the procedures as set forth in either paragraphs 6b, c, or d above, the new firm figure of POWs to be repatriated to the Communists would be supplied to them as soon as available. This figure would be submitted to them in the manner as set forth in paragraph 6b of reference D. The support of the public and our Allies for the United Nations Command position would have to be evaluated at this time to determine if delivery of the figure should be accompanied by an implied threat of a unilateral recess. This same factor would determine if we would at a later time unilaterally recess in the manner as set forth in paragraph 6c of reference D.

In presenting the proposal as set forth in paragraph 6, I feel that the following additional comments are pertinent:
a.
There is some security risk in allowing the neutral nations, sponsored by the enemy, to enter the rear areas and to visit POW camps while the war is still in progress. This concession might allow communication and contact with subversive pro-Communist elements in our rear, both in and out of our prisoner of war camps.
b.
The proposal as set forth in paragraphs 6b and c might be accepted by the Communists. Such a proposal might afford us some initial propaganda advantage. However, I consider their acceptance of such would be primarily to secure material for their propaganda machine rather than a real interest in the validity of the screening.
c.
Allowing representatives of the armed forces of the enemy in our rear areas, with no real indication of an armistice (as would be the case in proposal as set forth in paragraphs 6b and c) is, I believe, without precedent, and such concession might be employed by the Communists in their propaganda campaign.

I consider that there is a marginal advantage in having a neutral nation rather than the UNC itself initiate any proposal to accomplish a rescreening of the POWs by a group of neutral nations. Prior assurances of cooperation of other collaborating neutral nations and Communist-dominated nations should be secured before such a proposal is made by a neutral nation. Such a procedure would, in addition to avoiding long, drawn-out arguments between the United Nations Command and the Communists in present armistice sessions, also provide a means of testing their reaction to such a proposal and might provide some slight aspects of “face saving” for the Communists if they should elect to propose such a rescreening themselves. After such a proposal has been initiated by a neutral nation, a reasonable length of time should be allowed for the Communists to propose such in the armistice meetings. If they fail to do so, the United Nations Command Delegation would then propose such in the sequence as set forth in paragraph 6c above.
We are constantly seeking possible courses of action which may be employed in future negotiations. Will advise you of any courses of action which we consider practical.
